stage.name: Store buffer and alias checks: why load after store is special
stage.description: A load/store queue lesson on speculative stores, committed stores, LSU bypass, page-offset alias checks, and the no-forwarding caveat.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: Load after store is where memory gets serious

content.title: Store buffer and alias checks
content.subtitle: The hard part is not that loads and stores use memory. The hard part is knowing when a younger load might see stale data from an older store.
content.thesis: CVA6 keeps memory safe by tracking store intent and making certain loads wait instead of guessing.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The load unit checks the store buffer before issuing a load and stalls load-after-store cases when the older store has not reached the cache.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Stores can be speculative, so outstanding stores are tracked separately from committed stores; commit turns an outstanding store into a committed one.

content.speakerNotes: Now we zoom into the load-store queue discipline. The interesting case is not two independent memory operations. The interesting case is an older store followed by a younger load that might touch the same address. If the store has not safely reached memory, the load might read stale data. CVA6 handles that risk with store-buffer tracking and conservative stalls.

actions.text: Now we zoom into the load-store queue discipline. The interesting case is not two independent memory operations. The interesting case is an older store followed by a younger load that might touch the same address. If the store has not safely reached memory, the load might read stale data. CVA6 handles that risk with store-buffer tracking and conservative stalls.

scenes.title: The store buffer separates intent from effect

content.title: A store moves through two conceptual states
content.subtitle: Speculative store intent is not the same thing as a committed memory write.

steps.title: Store issued
steps.body: The store instruction enters the LSU path with transaction identity and address/data intent.

steps.title: Outstanding store
steps.body: The store is tracked while it is still speculative and may be flushed if the path is wrong.

steps.title: Commit signal arrives
steps.body: Commit turns the selected outstanding store into a committed store.

steps.title: Oldest committed store writes
steps.body: The committed side of the buffer tries to send the oldest store to the cache when possible.

content.takeaway: Do not say a store “executes” unless you also say which state it is in.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Stores can be speculative, so outstanding stores are tracked separately from committed stores; commit turns an outstanding store into a committed one.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: store buffer
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The store buffer tracks speculative and committed store queues; speculative entries are flushed while committed entries persist.

content.speakerNotes: The store buffer is the center of this lesson. A store can be issued and tracked before it is allowed to alter memory. The legacy design note describes separate speculative and committed queues. The CV32A65X design text says outstanding stores are differentiated from committed stores, and a commit event turns outstanding stores into committed stores. That is the discipline.

actions.text: The store buffer is the center of this lesson. A store can be issued and tracked before it is allowed to alter memory. The legacy design note describes separate speculative and committed queues. The CV32A65X design text says outstanding stores are differentiated from committed stores, and a commit event turns outstanding stores into committed stores. That is the discipline.

content.title: The LSU bypass FIFO decouples issue from slow memory decisions
content.body: Address generation, translation, and store-buffer checks can finish late. A small FIFO lets the LSU accept or hold a request without making the issue ready path painfully long.
content.caption: The LSU block diagram shows bypass, load unit, store unit, and memory-side structure.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: lsu_bypass
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The LSU bypass is a FIFO that keeps issue-stage requests when the load or store unit is not immediately available.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: LSU overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The legacy LSU note explains the DTLB, PTW, MMU, store buffer, load alias checks, and why loads can issue earlier than stores.

content.speakerNotes: The LSU bypass exists because memory decisions are timing-heavy. Address generation, translation, and store-buffer alias checking are not always cheap. If the issue stage had to wait on every late detail before seeing ready, the ready path would become unpleasantly long. The bypass FIFO is the small piece that keeps the machine disciplined without punishing the whole issue path.

actions.text: The LSU bypass exists because memory decisions are timing-heavy. Address generation, translation, and store-buffer alias checking are not always cheap. If the issue stage had to wait on every late detail before seeing ready, the ready path would become unpleasantly long. The bypass FIFO is the small piece that keeps the machine disciplined without punishing the whole issue path.

scenes.title: The page-offset check is a conservative filter

content.title: A younger load must respect older unresolved stores
content.subtitle: The load unit checks the store buffer before issuing the load to memory.

columns.title: Allowed without drama
columns.body: Two loads to the same address, two stores to the same address, and store-after-load cases do not require the same stale-data caution.

columns.title: Needs caution
columns.body: A load after a store to a matching page offset waits unless the older store has already been sent to the cache.

content.takeaway: The design prefers a stall over a silent stale read.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The load unit checks the store buffer before issuing a load and stalls load-after-store cases when the older store has not reached the cache.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: LSU overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The legacy LSU note explains the DTLB, PTW, MMU, store buffer, load alias checks, and why loads can issue earlier than stores.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the rule of thumb. A younger load is allowed only when it cannot accidentally read older stale data. The docs say the load unit checks the store buffer and stalls if a load-after-store hazard cannot be satisfied. The check uses page offset, because the page offset is the same before and after translation. It is conservative, but conservative is good when the alternative is quietly wrong.

actions.text: Here is the rule of thumb. A younger load is allowed only when it cannot accidentally read older stale data. The docs say the load unit checks the store buffer and stalls if a load-after-store hazard cannot be satisfied. The check uses page offset, because the page offset is the same before and after translation. It is conservative, but conservative is good when the alternative is quietly wrong.

scenes.title: No forwarding changes the mental model

content.title: Do not assume store-to-load forwarding exists

items.title: A matched older store blocks the younger load
items.body: If the store has not reached the cache, the load waits.

items.title: The store buffer owns freshness
items.body: The newest value may be in the store buffer, but this design does not forward it to the load.

items.title: Stall is correctness, not failure
items.body: Waiting preserves in-order memory visibility under the documented constraints.

items.title: Use source-scoped language
items.body: Say “for this documented CVA6 path” instead of generalizing to every processor.

content.takeaway: No forwarding makes the explanation simpler and the stall easier to justify.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The load unit checks the store buffer before issuing a load and stalls load-after-store cases when the older store has not reached the cache.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: LSU overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The legacy LSU note explains the DTLB, PTW, MMU, store buffer, load alias checks, and why loads can issue earlier than stores.

content.speakerNotes: Many processors implement store-to-load forwarding. This lesson is intentionally not making that assumption. The source text says there is no forwarding in this path, so a load after an unresolved matching store stalls. This is one of those places where being source-grounded matters. The safe teaching phrase is: in this documented CVA6 path, the design stalls rather than forwarding from the store buffer.

actions.text: Many processors implement store-to-load forwarding. This lesson is intentionally not making that assumption. The source text says there is no forwarding in this path, so a load after an unresolved matching store stalls. This is one of those places where being source-grounded matters. The safe teaching phrase is: in this documented CVA6 path, the design stalls rather than forwarding from the store buffer.

questions.question: What is the main reason stores need a buffer?

options.label: Stores can be speculative, but memory side effects must wait for commit discipline

questions.analysis: The store buffer separates speculative store intent from committed memory side effects.

questions.question: Why does the load unit check the store buffer?

options.label: To avoid reading stale data when an older store may target the same address
To choose the next fetch PC
To disable all loads after reset

questions.analysis: A younger load must not pass an unresolved older store that may produce the value it should observe.

questions.question: Which statements match the documented load/store behavior?

options.label: Two loads to the same address are allowed
Two stores to the same address are allowed in order
A load after an unresolved matching store may stall
A matched load always receives store-buffer forwarding

questions.analysis: The source explicitly describes conservative stall behavior and notes that forwarding is not done in this path.

questions.question: What does the LSU bypass mainly help with?

options.label: Decoupling issue from late LSU availability decisions

questions.analysis: The LSU bypass FIFO keeps requests when load/store units are not immediately available and helps avoid a long ready path.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Stores can be speculative, so outstanding stores are tracked separately from committed stores; commit turns an outstanding store into a committed one.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The load unit checks the store buffer before issuing a load and stalls load-after-store cases when the older store has not reached the cache.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: store buffer
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The store buffer tracks speculative and committed store queues; speculative entries are flushed while committed entries persist.

content.speakerNotes: This quiz should lock the queue model into place. Stores are tracked before they are committed. Loads check older stores before going to memory. No forwarding means the safe answer is often a stall. If those three points are clear, the next step is address translation: what happens when the address itself is virtual.

actions.text: This quiz should lock the queue model into place. Stores are tracked before they are committed. Loads check older stores before going to memory. No forwarding means the safe answer is often a stall. If those three points are clear, the next step is address translation: what happens when the address itself is virtual.

content.title: The store buffer is a correctness structure
takeaways: Outstanding stores and committed stores must be treated as different states.
A load-after-store alias check prevents stale reads.
The documented path stalls rather than relying on store-to-load forwarding.
content.nextStep: Next lesson: address translation, where virtual addresses meet TLBs, PTW, page faults, and Bare/Sv modes.
content.closing: If memory looks slow here, it is because correctness is doing real work.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: store buffer
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The store buffer tracks speculative and committed store queues; speculative entries are flushed while committed entries persist.

content.speakerNotes: The important idea is that the store buffer is not just a performance queue. It is a correctness structure. It separates speculative intent from committed effect. It gives loads a way to avoid stale reads. And in this documented path, it may choose waiting over fancy forwarding. Next we add virtual memory to the picture.

actions.text: The important idea is that the store buffer is not just a performance queue. It is a correctness structure. It separates speculative intent from committed effect. It gives loads a way to avoid stale reads. And in this documented path, it may choose waiting over fancy forwarding. Next we add virtual memory to the picture.
